# Executive Summary {-}

## Key Points {-}

- This report summarises analyses completed in the
  third year (2025/26) of the ?aayaaqa (Herring) Spawn
  Dynamics project.

- In 2024/25, population dynamics models were expanded to
  include spatial stock structure (three stocks corresponding
  to Stat Areas 23, 24, and 25), predation mortality from major
  herring predators, and age-dependent egg production. The
  three-stock model with predation (*SISCAH-3S-Pred*) was
  recommended as the most practical tool for evaluating
  management procedures.

- In 2025/26, a closed-loop simulation framework was
  developed for testing a potential WCVI herring re-opening
  procedure. The management procedure (MP) uses a two-step
  harvest control rule: (i) fit an aggregate single-stock
  assessment model to combined WCVI data and apply a minimum
  escapement rule to determine a coastwide total allowable
  catch (TAC), and (ii) distribute the TAC among Stat Areas
  based on recent spawn index proportions or other
  distribution methods.

- The MP was tested under three operating models (OMs) of
  increasing ecological complexity: (i) the *SISCAH-DDM*
  density-dependent mortality model, (ii) the *SISCAH-1S-Pred*
  single-stock model with explicit predation, and (iii) the
  *SISCAH-3S-Pred* three-stock model with explicit predation.
  The *SISCAH-3S-Pred* model serves as a robustness test,
  representing a scenario where the true population is
  spatially structured while the assessment model treats WCVI
  herring as a single stock.

- Under the *SISCAH-DDM* operating model, the MP performs as
  designed, maintaining biomass above the limit reference
  point ($0.3 B_0$) approximately 96\% of the time
  (exceeding the 75\% conservation objective) and producing
  catches
  near the maximum sustainable yield of approximately
  1,600 t.

- Under the *SISCAH-1S-Pred* operating model, catches are
  reduced and biomass declines toward the minimum escapement
  reference point ($B_{ref} = B_{MSY|DDM}$), reflecting the
  lower productivity expected when predation mortality is
  included.

- Under the *SISCAH-3S-Pred* operating model, the fishery is
  closed more than 50\% of the time because aggregate biomass
  is near the minimum escapement level. The spatial
  distribution method has very little effect on performance
  given the low catches in simulations.

- Three spatial TAC distribution methods were tested:
  (i) recent average spawn index proportions, (ii) recent
  average catch proportions, and (iii) directing 100\% of
  the TAC to Stat Area 23 (Barkley Sound). Differences among
  distribution methods were negligible under all operating
  models.

## Project Summary {-}

This project develops a Nuu-chah-nulth approach to
ecosystem-based fisheries management (EBFM) by integrating
life-history traits (e.g., age, size, fecundity) and spawning
dynamics (e.g., location, timing) in herring population models
for the West Coast of Vancouver Island (WCVI). These models
represent key assumptions about how herring populations
respond to changing ecosystem conditions and fisheries on a
finer spatial scale than is considered in current assessment
models for WCVI Pacific Herring.

This report summarises analyses conducted in the third year
(2025/26) of the ?aayaaqa (Herring) Spawn Dynamics project.
Building on the three-stock model with predation
(*SISCAH-3S-Pred*) developed in Phase 2, a closed-loop
simulation (management strategy evaluation) framework was
developed to test a proposed WCVI herring re-opening
procedure. The *SISCAH-3S-Pred* model is used as a
robustness test operating model, representing a scenario in
which the true population is spatially structured and subject
to predation mortality, while the management procedure uses
a simpler single-stock assessment. Results demonstrate how
assumptions about predation and spatial structure affect the
expected performance of harvest control rules and spatial TAC
distribution methods.
